does: This paper introduces GrACE, a method for confidence elicitation in large language models that operates by computing similarity between the final hidden state and a learnable special token embedding appended to the vocabulary. The approach fine-tunes models to calibrate confidence scores against accuracy targets, enabling real-time confidence assessment without additional sampling or auxiliary models. Extensive experiments demonstrate that GrACE achieves superior discriminative capacity and calibration compared to existing methods across open-ended generation tasks, while also enabling two novel confidence-based test-time scaling strategies that improve both reliability and efficiency in deployment scenarios.
connects: This work builds on the established problem of confidence calibration in neural networks, extending prior approaches that have struggled with the computational overhead of sampling-based methods or poor calibration properties of token probability baselines. GrACE responds to limitations in existing confidence elicitation techniques for LLMs, which either require expensive ensembling, multiple forward passes, or auxiliary models. The research connects to broader AI safety literature focused on uncertainty quantification and reliability assessment in high-stakes applications, building on calibration frameworks from classification settings and adapting them to the open-ended generation domain where traditional probability-based confidence metrics are less effective.
enables: This work enables practical deployment of LLMs in safety-critical domains by providing computationally efficient and well-calibrated confidence estimates. The proposed test-time scaling strategies leverage confidence scores to improve model outputs, suggesting directions for confidence-guided decoding and selective generation. Future work could extend GrACE to multimodal models, explore its application to other calibration-critical tasks beyond generation, or develop confidence-aware adaptation mechanisms that dynamically adjust model behavior based on expressed uncertainty. The framework also points toward hybrid approaches combining confidence elicitation with retrieval augmentation or external knowledge integration to further enhance reliability in high-stakes healthcare and financial applications.
